skill.name: wp-wpcli-and-ops
skill.description: Guides safe WordPress operations with WP-CLI, including migrations, database workflows, plugins, themes, cron, cache, multisite, and automation.
skill.summary: Run WordPress WP-CLI operations with safer targeting, backups, dry runs, and repeatable workflows.
skill.author: Automattic

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command patterns, but most Markdown backtick hits are WP-CLI documentation examples, not executable Ruby code. One helper script intentionally runs local WP-CLI through child_process, and the skill documents destructive WordPress operations, so publication is acceptable with a medium-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: WP-CLI Inspector Runs External Commands
medium_findings.description: The inspector imports node:child_process and calls spawnSync("wp", args). This is intended functionality and avoids a shell, but it still executes a local binary selected by PATH.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The external command execution is explicit and confirmed in the script. The use of argument arrays lowers injection risk, but the local wp binary still runs.

medium_findings.title: Documented Commands Can Mutate WordPress Data
medium_findings.description: The skill covers database import, search-replace, plugin updates, cache flushing, and bulk delete workflows. Guardrails are present, but misuse on production can cause data loss or downtime.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines directly list write operations and production cautions. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the same files require confirmation, backups, and dry runs.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Misclassified as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: Most static external command hits are inline Markdown examples of WP-CLI commands or flags. They are documentation for a WordPress operations skill, not executable Ruby backticks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is Markdown documentation, not source code execution syntax. The surrounding content consistently explains WP-CLI usage and safety steps.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged lines do not implement cryptography. They are plain prose about descriptions, safety rules, serialized data, and logging.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review found no hash, cipher, or cryptographic API usage at these locations. The scanner appears to have matched incidental character sequences in prose.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Findings Are Benign Troubleshooting Notes
low_findings.description: The flagged lines discuss WP-CLI troubleshooting or maintenance cautions. They do not gather host inventory, secrets, or network information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is operational guidance for WP-CLI commands. No evidence was found of broad system enumeration or collection of sensitive host details.

dangerous_patterns.title: Node child_process spawnSync
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper script uses spawnSync to run WP-CLI. This is expected for the skill, but users should trust the local wp executable and working environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly present in code and executes an external program. The impact depends on the local environment and PATH.

dangerous_patterns.title: Database and Content Mutation Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents WP-CLI commands that can change databases, URLs, plugins, themes, cache, rewrite rules, posts, and users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines list commands and workflows with direct write effects. The same documentation includes safeguards, so this is a controlled operational risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Production Bulk Update Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The package workflow includes update-all examples and warns against production use without a maintenance window.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Bulk update commands are present as examples and can affect live availability. The nearby guardrail reduces but does not remove operational risk.

content.value_statement: WordPress operations can damage content when commands target the wrong site. This skill gives safe WP-CLI workflows for migrations, updates, cron, cache, and multisite tasks.
actual_capabilities: Guides safe wp search-replace workflows with backups, dry runs, cache flushes, and rewrite flushes.
Helps inspect WP-CLI availability, WordPress install status, core version, multisite state, and site URLs.
Provides workflows for plugin, theme, cron, cache, rewrite, and multisite operations.
Shows guardrails for production changes, site targeting, environment checks, and maintenance windows.
Supports automation patterns using wp-cli.yml, shell scripts, and read-only CI checks.
limitations: Requires WP-CLI and WordPress files in the execution environment.
Cannot verify production safety unless the user provides environment and targeting details.
May print local site metadata in inspector output.
Does not replace backups, staging tests, or application health checks.

use_cases.title: Plan a Site URL Migration
use_cases.description: Prepare a safer search-replace sequence with backup, dry run, scoped execution, and verification steps.

use_cases.title: Debug Operational WordPress Issues
use_cases.description: Inspect WP-CLI setup, cron events, cache state, rewrite rules, and targeting problems before changing a site.

use_cases.title: Standardize Multisite Maintenance
use_cases.description: Create repeatable workflows for per-site or network-wide WP-CLI operations with clear safety controls.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect a Site
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the WP-CLI ops skill to inspect my WordPress site at --path=/var/www/html. Do not make changes.

prompt_templates.title: Plan URL Migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a safe WP-CLI URL migration from old.example.com to new.example.com for staging first. Include backup, dry run, and verification steps.

prompt_templates.title: Debug Cron and Cache
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me debug delayed WordPress cron jobs and stale page changes using WP-CLI. Avoid broad production actions unless I confirm them.

prompt_templates.title: Automate Multisite Maintenance
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a repeatable WP-CLI workflow for a multisite network that lists sites, targets each URL, logs commands, and requires an apply flag.

output_examples.input: I need to change a WordPress domain safely.
output_examples.output: A migration plan with environment confirmation, database backup, dry run, scoped replacement, cache flush, rewrite flush, and smoke tests.

output_examples.input: WP-CLI says WordPress is not installed.
output_examples.output: A troubleshooting checklist covering the site root, wp-config.php location, container context, and multisite URL targeting.

output_examples.input: I need to update plugins on production.
output_examples.output: A cautious maintenance workflow with inventory, backup, staging validation, maintenance window, targeted updates, rollback notes, and health checks.

best_practices: Confirm environment, path, URL, and multisite scope before any write operation.
Run backups and dry runs before database changes or search-replace operations.
Prefer read-only CI checks and require explicit apply flags for automated changes.
anti_patterns: Running production write commands without confirming the target site.
Using update-all or bulk delete commands without a maintenance window.
Skipping verification after cache, rewrite, database, plugin, or theme changes.

faq.question: Does this skill run WP-CLI automatically?
faq.answer: It can guide commands and includes an inspector script that runs local WP-CLI when the user invokes it.

faq.question: Is it safe for production sites?
faq.answer: It can be used for production planning, but write operations need confirmation, backups, dry runs, and a maintenance window.

faq.question: Does it support multisite?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for single-site targeting with --url and network-aware workflows.

faq.question: Can it migrate domains?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides a search-replace workflow with backup, dry run, scoped execution, and validation steps.

faq.question: What tools must be installed?
faq.answer: The environment needs WordPress files and WP-CLI available through the intended command runner.

faq.question: Does it handle backups?
faq.answer: It recommends WP-CLI database exports and backup checks, but users must store and verify backups themselves.
